coredata.dc:title: Dual lignin valorization enabled by carbon quantum dots and lithium-sulfur cathode
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jikun
Zhou, Pengfei
Yuan, Lan
Liu, Xinyan
Ma, Jianfeng
Zhang, Chuntao
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-related technologies hold great promise to tackle the double crises of environmental expense and energy scarcity. How to exert the trash-to-treasure was a strong driving force upon lignocellulose biorefinery that emphasizes a request for upgrading lignin. As a guiding light for two-in-one valorization of low-cost biomass, we reported a NaCl-templated strategy to convert well-defined lignin into porous carbon (LPC) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Besides, the affluent pores and channels of LPC can be easily penetrated by molten sulfur to assemble a cathode of lithium-sulfur battery. The LPC@S-installed cathode was able to provide a high sulfur utilization up to 66 wt%, stable cycling lifespan with an initial specific capacity of 1066 mA h g−1 at 1 C (capacity decay of 0.095 % per cycle over 400 cycles), and acceptable rate capability at a charge/discharge current from 0.1–3 C. The lignin-based CQDs exhibited an average size of 4.18 nm, along with bright fluorescence, long-term stability, and water solubility. Benefitting from these alluring properties, CQDs was efficiently served as the fluorescence-switchable nanoprobes for sensitively detecting common metal ions, which presented a significant fluorescence quenching in response to the presence of ferric iron (Fe3+) with a wide concentration range of 1–220 μM. It is reasonable to imagine that the present work delivers a versatile settlement to the end-up issue of technical lignin towards a circular material economy.

Structure characterization and NaCl-templated activation of lignin
Performance of lignin-derived porous carbon and Li-S cathode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a NaCl-templated strategy for the preparation of carbon quantum dots and porous carbon from lignin. LPC: lignin porous carbon; CQDs: lignin-based carbon quantum dots; LPC/S: the composite of lignin porous carbon and sulfur particles by one-pot molten sulfur strategy.

2D HSQC NMR spectra of lignin. Side-chain (a) and aromatic regions (b) in the HSQC NMR spectrum, δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 and δC/δH 100–150/6.0–8.0, respectively. Main classical structures of lignin (c): (A) β-O-4ʹ linkages; (B) resinol structures formed by β-βʹ/α-O-γʹ/γ-O-αʹ linkages; (C) phenylcoumarane structures formed by β-5ʹ/α-O-4ʹ linkages; (S) syringyl unit; (Sʹ) oxidized syringyl unit linked a carbonyl or carboxyl group at Cα (phenolic); (G) guaiacyl unit; (H) p-hydroxyphenyl units; (pCA) free p-coumaric acid. The scheme (d) of the conversion mechanism from lignin to porous carbon, including aromatic rebonding, refusion and deoxygenation reaction.

Structural characterization and sensing performance of CQD from lignin. (a) TEM images (scale bar 50 nm), (b) particle size distribution, high-resolution XPS spectra of carbon (c) and oxygen (d), (e) UV–vis absorption spectra, the emission spectrum obtained under maximum excitation at 360 nm, and the excitation spectrum observed at the maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm. Inset: photograph taken under 365 nm UV light. (f) Emission spectra recorded for progressively longer excitation wavelength in 20 nm increment from 300 nm to 420 nm. (g) Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of CQDs aqueous solutions in the presence of different metal ions, including Cr3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Al3+. (h) PL spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ in aqueous solution ranging from 0–220 μM.

Microscopic and physicochemical characterizations of lignin-derived porous carbon (LPC). SEM (a, scale bar 1 μm) and TEM (b, scale bar 50 nm; c, scale bar 5 nm) images, elemental mapping results, Raman spectra (d), TGA profile (e), XRD pattern (f), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (g), pore size distribution curve (h), and high-resolution XPS spectra of sulfur (i) of samples. Rational cell configuration (j) for LPC/S material in Li–S battery.

Electrochemical performances of lignin porous carbon assembled Li-S battery. (a) Cyclic voltammetry test (CV), (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), (c) discharge/charge curves observed at 0.2 C for 200 cycles, (d) rate performances at different current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 C, and (e) long-term cycling performance and corresponding coulombic efficiencies at 1 C for 400 cycles.

head.ce:title: Dual lignin valorization enabled by carbon quantum dots and lithium-sulfur cathode

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-related technologies hold great promise to tackle the double crises of environmental expense and energy scarcity. How to exert the trash-to-treasure was a strong driving force upon lignocellulose biorefinery that emphasizes a request for upgrading lignin. As a guiding light for two-in-one valorization of low-cost biomass, we reported a NaCl-templated strategy to convert well-defined lignin into porous carbon (LPC) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Besides, the affluent pores and channels of LPC can be easily penetrated by molten sulfur to assemble a cathode of lithium-sulfur battery. The LPC@S-installed cathode was able to provide a high sulfur utilization up to 66 wt%, stable cycling lifespan with an initial specific capacity of 1066 mA h g−1 at 1 C (capacity decay of 0.095 % per cycle over 400 cycles), and acceptable rate capability at a charge/discharge current from 0.1–3 C. The lignin-based CQDs exhibited an average size of 4.18 nm, along with bright fluorescence, long-term stability, and water solubility. Benefitting from these alluring properties, CQDs was efficiently served as the fluorescence-switchable nanoprobes for sensitively detecting common metal ions, which presented a significant fluorescence quenching in response to the presence of ferric iron (Fe3+) with a wide concentration range of 1–220 μM. It is reasonable to imagine that the present work delivers a versatile settlement to the end-up issue of technical lignin towards a circular material economy.

ce:section.ce:para: Next to cellulose, lignin is widely recognized as the indispensable natural polymer and amounting to 15∼30 wt % of plant biomass (Shuai et al., 2016; Zakzeski et al., 2010). Unfortunately, lignin is always been regarded as “the black sheep’’ and low-value byproduct in chemical pulping and/or lignocellulosic biorefinery. It is mostly combusted or slowly decayed, provoking a dual crisis of environmental pollution and wasteful resource (Habibi et al., 2010). The actual disdain over lignin is refused to encounter because of its inherent functions, such as protecting from insects and pathogens, enabling transport of water, and adding sturdiness and strength to plant cell walls (Moreno and Sipponen, 2020).

Unlike cellulose and hemicellulosic polysaccharides, lignin primarily consists of methoxylated phenylpropanoid subunits (Lobato-Peralta et al., 2021). It is synthesized mainly from three representative monomers, including sinapyl alcohol, coniferyl alcohol and p-coumaryl alcohol, through enzyme-guided dimerization, radical coupling, dehydrogenation reactions, which led to a three-dimensional (3D) heterogeneous complex interlinked with both carbon-carbon and aryl ether bonds (Wang et al., 2013). Thanks to that, an energy density of natural lignin is 30 % greater than that of polysaccharides, thereby guaranteeing lignin serves as a widely available source of renewable aromatics and drop-in fuels (Ragauskas et al., 2014; Rinaldi et al., 2016). Note that, it is difficult to interpret an original structure of lignin, owing to the outburst of molecular evolutions, sudden onset of resistive carbon-carbon bonds, and insertion of unexpected functional groups during various fractionation processes (Schutyser et al., 2018). Especially, the longstanding concern over lignin valorization is generally spread into advanced engineered materials, since the absence of applied lignin-first depolymerization strategies that efficiently manipulate aromatics while upgrading biomass polysaccharides.

The development of lignin-based nanoscopic and/or energy storage devices is gradually emerging as the next-generation of highly efficient and practical avenues in the field of biomass valorization. Up to now, the widespread efforts are dedicated to reform lignin towards versatile materials (Barhoum et al., 2020; Iravani and Varma, 2020; Yang et al., 2020a). For instance, the integration of electrospun lignin/polyacrylonitrile nanofiber electrodes and chemically cross-linked lignin hydrogel electrolytes was proposed to fabricate all-lignin-based flexible supercapacitors. The as-prepared devices exhibited excellent durability and flexibility under diverse bending angles, as well as, delivered a maximum energy and power density of 4.49 W h kg−1 and 2.63 kW kg−1 respectively (Park et al., 2019). A novel 3D hierarchical lignin-derived porous carbon (HLPC) prepared through ZnCO3 decomposition. As a result, an outstanding mass/volume energy density (550 mA h·g−1/730 mA h·cm−3 at 0.2 A·g−1 after 200 cycles) with approximately 99 % retention of HPLC was simultaneously outputted for lignin-based lithium-ion batteries (Xi et al., 2020). The sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (SCQDs) were easily prepared from hydrothermal treatment of lignin, which can be adopted as a sensor to detect Sudan I in acidic solutions with the linear range of 0–40 μM and limited detection of 0.12 μM (Yang et al., 2020b). A rapid microwave-aided processing of lignin was reported to synchronously prepare multi-scale nanomaterials, including nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots with bright purple photoluminescence, and monodisperse nanoparticles with high stability (Si et al., 2018). In light of this, how to integrate the superiority of lignin-based nano-materials and energy storage devices was a key point in the development of lignin valorization that heightens a two-in-one protocol.

Inborn gifts of short growth cycle, high productivity and easy access have widely propped natural bamboo as an energy crop in the spotlight of paper, textile, carbon materials and biorefinering (He et al., 2014; Peng and She, 2014). In the context of scale-up bamboo valorization, it is emergent to broaden the knowledge of multi-scale lignin functionality. It is also generally appreciated that the research emphasis has been scarcely paid to the simultaneous fabrication of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and lithium-sulfur (Li-S) cathode from lignin. Herein, we reported a NaCl-template pathway to fulfill a goal of “two birds with one stone” from lignin that formed porous carbon (LPC) and fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). An in-depth structural analysis of lignin was conducted to bridge a gap between macromolecular framework (i.e., lignin composition, substructures, inter-unit linkages, and hydroxyls) and physicochemical performances of lignin-based carbon materials (e.g., CQD and LPC). Encouragingly, the as-prepared CQDs possessed quasi-spherical morphology, bright blue fluorescence, and natural hydrophilicity, which can be efficiently utilized as nano-probe for the selective detection of ferric iron (Fe3+) present in aqueous phase. Through a traditional melt-diffusion method with sulfur particles, the LPC can be facilely installed as a Li-S cathode with an excellent electrochemical performance. A potential deduction was also proposed to bring a substantial assistance on the bridge of lignin structure with performances of CQD and Li-S cathode. In short, the novel integration of lignin-derived CQDs and energy storage devices facilities the valorization of lignin into multidimensional products, which could concurrently maximize the energy and environmental benefits of biorefinery industry. This method opened a brand-new vision for two-in-one valorization of lignin resources in the double fields of metal ions sensing and cell electrode.

We collected bamboo as lignocellulosic feedstock from Yunnan province, China. The bamboo culm was initially ground to obtain the 20–80 mesh particles, and then oven-dried at 60 °C for 48 h. Alkaline lignin was isolated from the ball-milled bamboo with 1% sodium hydroxide with a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1 : 10 (g mL−1) at 80 °C for 4 h. The resulted supernatant was neutralized to pH 5.5 with acetic acid, and then concentrated by distillation under a vacuum condition. After deposition of hemicelluloses by using 95 % ethanol, bamboo lignin can be easily isolated through rotary evaporation (concentrated to about 20–30 ml) and acidic water (pH 2.0) precipitation. All the chemical reagents used are of analytical grade or the best available.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lignin-derived CQDs and Li-S cathode
ce:section.ce:para: In a NaCl-templated synthesis, the self-stacking of water-soluble NaCl crystals was employed to generate a hard template for the 3D lignin@NaCl network. After carbonization, the LPC can be successfully formed by eliminating NaCl template (Supporting information S1.1). The CQDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal processing of lignin porous carbon, which was mixed with deionized water (Supporting information S1.2). An array of techniques was conducted to make a structural characterization of lignin, LPC and CQDs (Supporting information S1.3 and S1.4). The LPC/S cathode of Li-S cell was prepared via a facile melt-diffusion method from lignin porous carbon and sublimed sulfur. The mass loading of sulfur into each cathode was around 1.9 mg cm−2. Also, the physicochemical properties and electrochemical capabilities of Li-S cathode (LPC/S) were studied (Supporting information S1.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and NaCl-templated activation of lignin
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram for the dual preparation of CQDs and LPC/S from lignin is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Herein, alkaline lignin was explored as a low-cost precursor that can also be potentially available from pulping and biorefinering industries on a large-scale. Enabled by illustrating the interplay between lignin structures with product properties (i.e., CQDs and LPC@S), it is reasonable to maneuver a prelude of scaling-up industrial lignin in the dual realms of environmental and energy materials. NaCl and lignin were firstly dispersed in deionized water to produce a well-distributed solution, which was subsequently subjected to freeze-drying. During the freezing process, the recrystallization of self-assembly NaCl crystals was taken place in a restricted size to serve as a template for homogeneously coating with an ultrathin lignin film, thereby forming the hybrid structure of lignin@NaCl. Upon heating at a given temperature of 700 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere, the lignin underwent carbonization to fabricate a complex substance of NaCl crystals with a finite morphology and graphitic carbon with micro- and mesopores (Li et al., 2016). Finally, the immersion of LPC@NaCl in deionized water was conducted to completely dissolve the NaCl template, thereby creating macropores in the composite. During this process, the soluble NaCl salt not only served as a hard template to generate hierarchically porous structure of the as-prepared carbon (Yan et al., 2020), but also promoted the carbon graphitization degree (Ding et al., 2015).

Note that, the discrepancy in reactivity, composition, and structural linkages of diverse lignin subunits could provoke a cascade behavior of carbon matrix and surface functional groups. In this vein, we need to fill a gap in the crucial juncture between lignin molecular structure and its derived carbon materials. Alkaline fractionation using dilute NaOH is a typical lignocellulosic deconstruction to isolate lignin, in which the efficient cleavage of ester linkages between xylan and lignin along with the breakdown of the β-O-4′ lignin inter-units are the immediate triggers for lignin dissolution (Moodley et al., 2020). Thus, a carbohydrate-rich fraction was produced to act as a paramount substrate for biofuel production. As shown in Fig. S1, the average molecular weight (M w) and polydispersity index (PDI) of alkaline lignin are 2500 g/mol and 1.92, respectively. The numerical value of PDI, calculating from the ratio of M w/M n, represents the heterogeneous distribution of lignin molecular size. Also, the M w of lignin displayed a potential role in the properties and boundaries of carbon cores. The beginning-to-end information on the structural and compositional features of the lignin macromolecules, such as basic subunits and linkages, could be recognized by 2D HSQC spectra. The aliphatic region between δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0, indicating the main inter-unit linkages and branched chains of lignin, can be observed in the Fig. 2 a. The isolated lignin was clearly found to be dominated by β-aryl ether linkages (β-O-4′, A) accompanying with a small amount of resinol (β-βʹ, B) and phenylcoumaran (β-5ʹ, C) substructures (Wang et al., 2020). A highly linear correlation between lignin β-O-4ʹ linkages with crystallite structure/physicochemical performances of lignin-based carbon materials has been verified by a recent report (Li et al., 2021). More importantly, such a well-defined interplay could guide the precise design of chemical processing of lignin molecular structures toward high-quality lignin-based multicarbon materials. With a directed control of high β-O-4ʹ linkages in lignin, the cellulose bioeconomy of both lignocellulosic biorefinery and pulping industry could be thus reformed by maximizing the revenues of high-value production from lignin-derived carbon materials (Li et al., 2021). The methoxyl associated with specific lignin aromatics (e.g., syringyl and guaiacyl) was also easily separated from the side-chain region of lignin, which is likely facilitated to form a porous structure via carbonization (Li et al., 2018b). The response of C–H correlations in β-d-xylopyranoside (xylan) was also located in the aliphatic region of lignin, suggesting the existence of associated sugars in the lignin branches. The aromatic region of lignin spectra ranging from δC/δH 100–150/6.0–8.0, which corresponded to the basic phenylpropanoid subunits, is presented in Fig. 2b. The cross-signals from syringyl (S), Cα-oxidized syringyl (S'), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H) lignin motifs were sharply differentiated in the aromatic regions (Shen et al., 2020). A recent publication reported that p-coumaric acid (pCA) is always acylated at C-γ of side chain in grass lignin (Wen et al., 2015). The prominent signals of pCA provided a direct proof to the survival of p-coumaric acid under the mild alkaline condition. The cardinal motifs of lignin and their related assignment of 1H–13C cross-signals are presented in Fig. 2c and Table S1, respectively.

The 31P NMR technique is usually employed as a precise method to quantify the main hydroxyl groups (e.g., −OH and −COOH) that display a crucial role in the surface decorations of carbon matrix (Xu et al., 2020b). From Fig. 2d, the contents of aliphatic OH, phenolic OH (total P−OH), and COOH presented in lignin molecules were 3.132 mmol/g, 3.009 mmol/g, and 0.521 mmol/g respectively. The dominant species of phenolic hydroxyls appeared to be syringyl OH (SOH, 1.087 mmol/g), followed by guaiacyl OH (GOH, 0.607 mmol/g). A relatively lower content of p-hydroxyphenyl OH (HOH, 0.458 mmol/g) was also determined in lignin, which is highly consistent with the typical features of bamboo lignin. The condensed−OH content of CSOH and CGOH was 0.154 mmol/g and 0.182 mmol/g respectively, which potentially provided an evidence for the low-level occurrence of lignin condensation during alkaline extraction. It should be noted that the existence of oxygen-contained groups (e.g., OCH3, OH, and COOH) in lignin precursor would in-situ doping into LPC, which is beneficial for generating active spots (e.g., intrinsic defect, oxygen-excited vacancy and edge), improving electrical conductivity, endowing surface wettability, and expanding the interlamellar spacing of carbon-based materials (Wu et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2020). Also, the surface chemistry and photoluminescence features of CQDs generated from LPC can be affected by the oxygen-contained groups of lignin.

During pyrolysis, the weight loss of lignin was mainly ascribed to the release of phenolic volatiles, methane, CO2, CO, H2O (Wang et al., 2009). We proposed the tentative mechanism of transforming lignin into micron-sized porous carbon (LPC), which is schematically depicted in Fig. 2e. It has mainly consisted of three stages: 1) aromatic rebonding, 2) refusion and 3) deoxygenation reaction. According to the bonding sites of lignin labeled in 2D-HSQC NMR spectra, it has been well acceptable that the lignin phenolic OH structures, such as pCA, S, G, and H, were the main reactive sites of lignin (Ding et al., 2020). During NaCl-templated carbonization, the aromatic condensation could be facilitated with G2, G5 and G6 sites. A considerable amount of pCA motifs up to 5% in grass feedstock (Sun et al., 2002), serving as the reactive center to produce ordered sp 2-hybridized aromatic rings, could be readily exposed under alkaline environment due to the alkali-sensitive ester bonds (Buranov and Mazza, 2008; Ding et al., 2020). Besides, the methoxy groups offered a relatively higher oxygen content of lignin, which favored the formation of active sites, branched and condensed structure during carbonization (Li et al., 2018b).

As an emerging building unit, CQDs have been igniting the renovation in the domains of biomedicine, sensing, optoelectronics, and bioimaging (Wang et al., 2021a). It is worth mentioning that lignin seems to be attractive for preparing CDs because of its high carbon content and aromatic phenylpropane structure, which provides a powerful benefit of resembling the carbon skeleton of CDs. Herein, we developed a facile hydrothermal processing of LPC to acquire highly luminescent CQDs with water solubility and stable fluorescence emission. The morphology of lignin-based CQDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which is presented in Fig. 3 a. The well-dispersion of quasi-spherical CQDs without obvious aggregation is observed from TEM image. High-resolution TEM image exhibits well-resolved fringes 0.21 nm apart due to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon (a lattice distance of 0.21 nm), implying that the particles had highly crystalline carbon structures (Li et al., 2018a). The CQDs are distributed in the range 2–9 nm with average diameter 4.18 nm (Fig. 3b), affirming a uniform distribution responsible for the boundary and photoluminescence of CQDs. The structural aspects affecting the luminescence of CQDs was further explored by the employment of high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) surface detection, as illustrated in Fig. 3c-d. The C 1s pattern has three component peaks corresponding to sp 2 carbon (CC, 284.8 eV), sp 3 carbon (CO, 285.9 eV), and carbonyl groups (CO, 288.7 eV) (Wang et al., 2021a). It was also reported that the CQDs mainly consisted of carbon cores with π-conjugated domains surrounded by amorphous surface regions, and that the influences of particle size and sp 2/sp 3 hybridization ratio are also of great concern (Wang et al., 2021a). The three-component O 1s peaks at 532.3, 533.1 eV and 535.8 stemmed from CO, C–O and OCO, respectively. The O 1s spectrum indicates that the accumulation of CO bonds in CQDs was corresponded to the degree of oxidation. Overall, the nano-sized CQDs have extensively conjugated sp 2 domains including sufficient oxygen-related surface groups (e.g., OCH3, COOH and OH), which is highly related with their PL emission.

In aqueous solution, the optical properties of the as-prepared CQDs were determined by UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3e, the location of strong absorption peak was observed at ∼265 nm which is triggered by π-π* transition of the conjugated CC/CO bonds in sp 2 hybridized carbon core (Dong et al., 2013; Hoan et al., 2019). The inset photograph of Fig. 3e exhibits that the color of lignin-based CQDs solution was quickly altered from faint yellow in natural light to bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp of wavelength 360 nm. In response to 360 nm excitation, a maximum forceful band at 440 nm with a Stokes shift of 80 nm is presented in the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3e). The PL excitation indicated that the strongest absorption of CQDs occurred in the range of 400–460 nm, which is analogous to the documented PL excitation analysis of carbon dots (Hoan et al., 2019). The CQDs displayed an excitation peak centered at about 449 nm, which might be attributed to specific diameter, carbon core and surface structure (de Oliveira and da Silva Abreu, 2021). Here, the hybridization degree of carbon core and its oxygen species might be the key driving force for CQDs fluorescence via the adjustment of core electronic environment (Jana et al., 2019). The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at diverse excitation wavelengths with 20 nm increment ranging from 300 nm to 420 nm are presented in Fig. 3f. Benefiting from the quantum confinement effect, the CQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission behavior that is a common pattern of carbon dots (Kurian and Paul, 2021). Upon the excitation wavelength at 360 nm, the CQDs had a maximum fluorescence intensity. Accompanied by the increase of excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission slowly shifted towards a longer wavelength and its intensity gradually decreased. The floating position of CQDs’ emission peak was observed while increasing the excitation wavelength, which implied that the fluorescence mechanism of size-dependent interband transitions outplayed surface defect states (Lim et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2021).

As CQDs are emerging as a new class of fluorescent sensors owing to their unique optical properties, good solubility, small size, chemical inertness, photo-stability, and biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2020a). We speculated that lignin-functionalized CQDs would be applied to optically detect ferric ions (Fe3+), as it is known the aromatic ring skeleton of lignin phenylpropane subunits has an indispensable binding affinity to Fe3+ (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014). In this vein, the selective fluorescence recognition of heavy metal ions on lignin-based CQDs was explored to monitor the quality of environmental water by biomass nanoprobe. Different species of typical heavy metal ions, including Cr3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Al3+ of each at the concentration of 400 μM, were uniformly mixed with as-prepared CQDs aqueous solution of 0.50 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 3g, the PL intensity suggested that the highly selectivity of CQDs upon Fe3+ was obviously determined from normalized PL intensity, while other metal ions cannot be able to exert significant quenching effect relative to the free CQDs. Besides, the competition test was conducted by adding 400 μM Fe3+ to the aqueous solution of CQDs in the presence of 400 μM other metal ions. As expected, a negligible difference in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was detected after mixing with any of interference metal ions.

As can be seen from Fig. 3h, the fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ exhibited a dose-dependent behavior, and the response spans over a wide concentration range with 0–220 μM. It is conceivable that Fe3+ ion acted as a coordinating center to bridge several lignin-based GQDs together and quenching occurred from the as-above induced aggregation of CQDs. This PL quenching is potentially guided by the non-radiative electron transfer that involves partial shift from an excited electron state to the d orbital of Fe3+. As compared with the other metal ions, it is reasonable to postulate that the CQDs responsible for Fe3+ recognition are partly ascribed to the synergistic action of quick chelating kinetic and robust affinity interaction between Fe3+ with COOH and/or OH groups on the surface of CQDs (Zhang et al., 2014). In addition, the adsorption of Fe3+ on the surface of CQDs enabled by the coordination between Fe3+ with COOH/OH groups on the edge of CQD (Qu et al., 2013), which led to a severe aggregation of CQDs with each other. Affecting by the coordination interaction, it is certain that the transformation of CQDs-derived excited-state electron into the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ has taken place for the sake of non-radiative electron/hole recombination, which led to a significant fluorescence quenching (Liu et al., 2017). These findings would open a window for the widespread applications of such emerging 0D nanoscopic materials in pollutant monitoring system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of lignin-derived porous carbon and Li-S cathode
ce:section.ce:para: The ever-spreading energy requirement stimulates the pursuit of developing biomass-derived electrode materials for rechargeable batteries on account of their virtues, such as low-cost, resource abundance, nontoxicity, and tunable electrochemistry (Wang et al., 2021b). Of note, the respective bottlenecks are still existed in lignocellulose-based Li-S cathode that need to be alleviated for their commercial applications, such as limited theoretical capacity, low conductivity, polysulfide shuttle effect and sluggish reaction kinetics. As next-generation energy storage devices, Li–S batteries are broadly developed to endow a low mass density (i.e., 0.534 g cm−3 of Li and 2.07 g cm−3 o f S), high theoretical capacity (i.e., 3860 mA h g−1 of Li and 1675 mA h g−1 of S), and environmental superiority (Yao et al., 2020). To meet the success of lignin-involved Li-S cell, Fig. 4 a-c display the SEM, TEM and, elemental mapping results of lignin-derived porous carbon with sulfur loading. Through the modulation of a NaCl template, the loose 3D interconnected macroporous framework was conferred to LPC that also had a porous size distributed from nanometers to micrometers (Fig. 4a). Due to the cubic crystal of NaCl salt, the configuration of LPC macropores had legible corners and edges, which is differed from the irregularly circular pores stemming from the releasing gases during pyrolysis (Fig. 4a-b) (Shi et al., 2018). This phenomenon might have a positive action regarding the permeation of electrolyte into almost the whole active materials, as well as, the minimization of ions diffusion distances from electrolyte to electrode (Lu et al., 2018). Through heating the mixture of LPC and commercial crystalline sulfur at 155 °C, the encapsulation of sulfur into the LPC matrix was successfully carried out. As shown in Fig. 4c of LPC@S, no obvious aggregation of sulfur particles around or on the surface of LPC was observed. Enabled by the strong affinity of sulfur with carbon, the molten sulfur gradually dispersed into the space between the interlayers of LPC and subsequently diffused into the nanopores of LPC during the impregnation process (Ding et al., 2013). After that, sulfur was entirely transformed into amorphous form in the LPC@S composite by solidifying in the nanopores (Ding et al., 2013). Due to the pore volume and large surface area of the hydrophobic carbon matrix of LPC with the aid of forceful capillary forces, sulfur could be efficiently implanted inside the channels (Ji et al., 2009, 2011). The elemental mapping images of carbon and sulfur show an intensity-alike distribution, indicating the homogeneous permeation of sulfur particles into the pores of LPC. It was further demonstrated that the homogeneous distribution of sulfur particles throughout the composite interior and/or surface, which affords an accessible surface for chemical adsorption of polysulfides. In one word, it can be postulated that the LPC@S composite is beneficial for the ingress and/or egress of electrolyte within a 3D hierarchically porous structure, thereby escalating the kinetics in the Li-S electrode.

Broadly speaking, the relative graphitization degree of carbon-based materials can be evaluated by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 4d displays the Raman spectra of LPC/S and LPC, in which the response of D and G bands is centered at two relatively wide peaks of 1320 and 1590 cm−1 respectively. Of note, the D band is generally stemmed from the sp 3 disorders and defects in the graphitic structure. By contrast, the appearance of G band can be provoked by the E 2g in-plane vibration of sp 2-linked graphitic carbon atoms (Shi et al., 2018). The intensity ratio of I D to I G was generally calculated to consider the graphitization degree of carbon materials comprising amorphous state (Shi et al., 2018). The I D/I G values of LPC/S and LPC are 1.73 and 1.59, respectively, implying a slightly higher graphitization degree of LPC than that of LPC. The decreased graphitization degree of LPC/S could be attributed to the intercalation of molten sulfur particle into carbon matrix. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was plotted to quantify the actual sulfur in the LPC/S composite. From Fig. 4e, a sulfur loading of LPC/S is quantified to be as high as about 66 %. It is convinced that the slight elevation of sulfur evaporation temperature has a direct relationship with the absorption behavior of sulfur into LPC matrix. Fig. 4f presents the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of LPC and LPC/S samples. The XRD spectra of LPC/S mainly depicts a visible characteristic peak at 44° and a broad diffraction peak at around 24°, which can be triggered by the (100) and (002) graphitic facets of carbonaceous materials with a high graphitization nature, respectively (Pan et al., 2017). After filling sulfur into the LPC host, the XRD curves of LPC/S composite confirmed the orthorhombic structure of amorphous carbon and sulfur, potentially revealing that the crystalline structure of active sulfur was largely maintained during diffusion treatment. Of note, the successful intercalation of sublimated sulfur into the porous loci of LPC can be affirmed by a fact, that is, the peak strength of crystalline sulfur became weak relatively. The classical characteristics of I- and IV-type isotherms with well-known plateaus between P/P 0 of 0.1–1.0 and an obvious hysteresis loop at the P/P 0 > 0.3 are clearly observed from the N2-adsorption isotherms of LPC (Fig. 4g), which suggested that both micropores and mesopores coexisted in bulk LPC with a BET specific surface area of 1087 m2 g−1. The wide electrochemical active interfaces enabled by a high BET surface area of LPC to favor the redox of sulfur species (Xie et al., 2019). The calculation of pore size distribution from the isotherms via BJH models is shown in Fig. 4h. The mesoporosity of LPC could be explicitly recognized with dominant mesopore signals ranging from 2 to 8 nm in diameter with a total pore volume of 1.13 m3 g−1, which is a favorable environment for accommodating the sulfur species, decreasing the ion transfer impedance, and relieving volume change (Xie et al., 2019). It is generally by now believed that the micropores between 0.8 and 1.4 nm are fully accessible to transport electrolyte anions, dropping a hint that LPC is competent for Li-S cathode (Shi et al., 2018). Moreover, the interconnected macropores of LPC could be acted as ion-buffering reservoirs to shorten the diffusion distances of electrolyte ions to the interior surfaces (Shi et al., 2018). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also adopted to survey the elemental status of LPC/S, regarding with the chemical connection between carbon host and active sulfur. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p region (Fig. 4i) exhibits three well-defined peaks at 164.2, 166.8, and 169.7 eV, corresponding to S 2p 3/2, S 2p 1/2, and sulfate respectively. From a recent report (Tang et al., 2018), the location of binding energies of elemental sulfur were presented at around 163 eV and 165 eV for S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2, respectively. It was noted that the binding energies of S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 slightly shifted to a lower status, suggesting the conceivable presence of CS bonds responsible for the interaction between sulfur within carbon matrix (Wang et al., 2014). The hierarchically porous structure of LPC enabled suitable pore size distribution and large specific surface area, which is expected to reduce the diffusion distance of electrolyte ions from the surface to interior of electrode and have an excellent capacitive performance of Li-S battery (Lu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2012).

Rational cell configuration using LPC/S cathode and metallic Li anode in Li-S battery is depicted in Fig. 4j. The electrochemical performance of LPC/S as Li-S cathode was assessed by a series of techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic charge/discharge tests. From Fig. 5 a of CV curve, a pair of prominent peaks appeared at around 2.01 and 2.32 V are assigned to the restore process from cyclo-sulfur to long-chain lithium polysulfides (Li2S4–8) and then to the lower polysulfides (Li2S2/Li2S) during the discharging process, respectively. In the opposite, the peak around 2.42 V arose from the oxidation of Li2S2/Li2S to Li2S4–8 and/or S8 (Chen et al., 2019). Delightfully, the overlapping of subsequent CV profiles of LPC/S is indicative of an excellent cycling stability. The oxidation peak of first cycle exhibits a slightly lower voltage potential, suggesting the potential activation for the formation of Li2Sn (n > 2) and/or sulfur (from Li2S2/Li2S). As shown in Fig. 5b, the EIS properties of the LPC/S electrode were investigated before and after the cycling. The initial Nyquist plot displays a depressed semicircle in the high-frequency range that involved in charge transfer resistance (R ct) before the cycling (Kim et al., 2019). After the cycling, the Nyquist plot shows two semicircles in the high-frequency region, which is potentially related with a passivation film deriving from insoluble polysulfides (Li2S2 or Li2S) (Kim et al., 2019). The LPC/S electrode still remains a lower R ct value after 400 cycles, providing the synergistic benefit for stable electrochemical performances. The charge and discharge curves of the LPC/S electrode at 0.2 C are further plotted (Fig. 5c). Two voltage plateaus in the discharge curves of LPC/S electrode are highly consistent with the CV curve. Meanwhile, the charge/discharge capacity delivered 1013/1118 mA h g−1 (coulombic efficiency up to 90.6 %) for the LPC/S electrode during the first cycle at 0.2 C. We observed that the overpotential of LPC/S (ΔE) cathode was steady after 200 cycles, which had a bearing on the redox reaction kinetics of lithium polysulfides during charge/discharge process (Chen et al., 2019). The rate performances of LPC/S was also studied at various current densities from 0.1–3 C (Fig. 5d). The discharge capacities of LPC/S were determined to be 1298, 1082, 904, 823, 746, and 643 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 C (1 C = 1672 mA h g−1) respectively, displaying impressive rate capability and specific capacity. The reversible capacity of 1060 mA h g−1 can be persisted while returning the current density from 3 to 0.1 C, implying excellent stability of the LPC/S electrode at various rates. Long-term cycling performance of the LPC/S electrode at 1 C is summarized in Fig. 5e. The coulombic efficiency of the LPC/S was up to 97–100 % after five cycles, indicative of excellent cycle performance. The LPC/S electrode exhibited an initial discharge capacity of 1066 mA h g−1, which maintained 659 mA h g−1 after 400 cycles with a relatively higher coulomb efficiency over 98 %. The average attenuation per cycle reached as low as 0.095 %. As-mentioned positive features indicated that LPC/S from lignin using a NaCl-template exhibited an acceptable discharge capacity with excellent cycle performance. Commercial lignin-derived carbon with macro-/micro-porous structure has been successfully prepared to play the part of Li-S cathode that maintained a specific value of 791.6 mA h g−1 in the 100th cycle at 0.5 C (Yu et al., 2017). Through the hydrothermal carbonization, KOH activation and heteroatom-doping, a nitrogen-incorporated honeycomb-like nanoporous carbon (n-hC) was synthesized from lignin precursor. The S@n-hC achieved a high initial discharge capacity of 1295.5 mA h g−1 at 0.1 C and retained 647.2 mA h g−1 after 600 cycles, and possessed excellent cycling stability (with the capacity fading of 0.05 % per cycle over 900 cycles at 1 C) (Yeon et al., 2020). A lightweight and binder-free Li-S electrode was enabled by lignin fibers@carbon-nanotubes and graphene, which provided an outstanding discharge capacity as high as 1632 mA h g−1 at 0.1 C and a specific capacity of 987 mA h g−1 after 500 cycles at 1 C benefiting from the efficient polysulfide-anchoring performance of lignin fiber, 3D conductive frameworks of CNTs, and depolarization effect of graphene (Liu et al., 2018). Although the electrochemical performance of LPC/S cathode was not extraordinary as compared with carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotubes), we also have a steadfast faith in the present work that stimulates the dual revival of disposal lignin residues from pulping and biorefinerying industries via a facile strategy to spread a waste-free standpoint. And best of, a special tale of environmental sensing and energy storage is devoted to maneuver lignin that often hinders modern biomass technologies.

Efficient valorization of lignin dictates the economic success and technical boom of modern biorefinery industry. Lignin, a renewable aromatic biopolymer, is one of the most alluring natural precursors for task-tailored materials capable of responding to nanotechnology and energy storage. From an easily available lignin with high abundance of β-aryl ether and hydroxyl groups, we presented a synchronous tactic to prepare porous active carbon and carbon quantum dots via a NaCl-induced approach. Due to the bright fluorescence, stable monodispersion, and uniform quasi-spherical structure with functional groups, lignin-based CQDs are potential to be excellent nanoprobes for the selective recognition and sensing of Fe3+ ions. As a cathode in Li-S cell, LPC/S delivers an initial discharge capacity of 1066 mA h g−1, which still preserves 659 mA h g−1 after 400 cycles at 1 C with a coulombic efficiency over 98 %. In terms of versatile biorefiney, lignin readily attained a novel fusion of CQDs and Li-S cathode through a facile NaCl-template, which is of particular advantage for boosting lignin valorization.

Jikun Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Pengfei Zhou: Formal analysis, Investigation. Lan Yuan: Writing - review & editing. Xinyan Liu: Writing - review & editing. Jianfeng Ma: Writing - review & editing, Resources. Chuntao Zhang: Resources, Project administration.
